63a5146d0af62c6119349e63.question: One poem by this author relates how Betty flies from her master’s bed to muss up her own, and “schoolboys lag with satchels in their hands” while debt-collectors gather in front of his lordship’s gate. Another poem by this author describes how the titular phenomenon causes “Triumphant Tories and despondent Whigs” to “forget their feuds and join to save their wigs,” and also notes that the title weather event carries “Drown’d puppies…dead cats, and Turnip-Tops.” Another poem by this author of “A Description of the Morning” and “A Description of a City-Shower” discusses how the title character employs “various combs for various uses” and describes how looking into a chest resulted in an odor spewing forth as if from “Pandora’s box / when Epimetheus oped the locks.” That poem by this author describes how Strephon sneaks into the title locale only to discover in horror that “Oh! Celia, Celia, Celia shits!” For 10 points, name this poet of “The Lady’s Dressing Room,” who also wrote some Drapier’s Letters and A Tale of a Tub.
